desc: Livno is a high karst plain town that feels more like an inland staging post for huge sky, pasture and mountain drives than a conventional sightseeing stop. People come for the wild horses on the Kruzi plateau, the Duman spring and the plateau scenery, then stay long enough to notice the cheese culture, Ottoman and Austro-Hungarian layers, and the unusual calm of western Herzegovina's upland edge.
hls: Access to Glamoč and Kupres roads

todo.name: Kruzi plateau wild horses
todo.desc: Book a responsible local drive to see the free-roaming horse herds without pressuring the animals.

todo.name: Duman spring
todo.desc: Walk to the strong karst spring below town for a compact sense of Livno's landscape.

todo.name: Old town viewpoints
todo.desc: Explore the slopes above town for mosque, church and valley views in one quiet circuit.

todo.name: Local cheese tasting
todo.desc: Seek out Livno cheese and simple highland cooking as part of the trip, not just a snack.

todo.name: Gorica and Franciscan museum
todo.desc: Add the monastery complex and museum if you want more context than just the horse-and-landscape side of Livno.

todo.name: Kamesnica or Cincar panorama drive
todo.desc: Use a longer mountain drive for the full sense of how exposed and spacious the Livno field really is.

wifi.note: Usually workable in central hotels, cafes and apartments, but plateau excursions, horse-viewing drives and smaller guesthouses can be much less dependable.

prac.fltNote: Livno itself is reached by road rather than by flying into the destination. Split is often the easiest UK gateway and easyJet is currently selling London Gatwick-Split nonstop flights, while Sarajevo makes more sense if the wider trip is Bosnia-focused and Mostar remains the closest Herzegovina airport with a much thinner airline list. The main planning question is road time over high ground, not airport distance alone.
prac.lang: Bosnian/Croatian/Serbian; English varies outside major tourist areas
prac.visa: British citizens can travel to Bosnia and Herzegovina without a visa for up to 90 days within a 6-month period, and GOV.UK says your passport must be less than 10 years old and valid for at least 90 days after the date you plan to leave.
alerts: Wild-horse visits should use responsible local guides and keep distance.
Winter roads over high ground can be snow-affected.
Public transport is limited, so car planning matters.
Do not expect a polished tourist-town evening scene once day driving is done.

sweet: May, June and September are usually the best balance for horse-plateau outings, road conditions and cooler walking weather than the full summer heat.
